facts_disclosed: Switched to Bıyıklı pomade, 95 lira/tin, Erkek supplier closed

turns.content: my friend... I have a headache today. not from the head, from the business. everything is changing and I cannot keep up
Sorry to hear that. What's going on — is it a specific problem or just a lot of things piling up?

a specific thing... but it is connected to everything. you know in my shop, the products I use... they are not just products. the pomade, the soap, the cologne... these are part of the identity. my customers come and they expect a certain smell, a certain feel. it is like... Gewohnheit, you know, habit. they are used to it

Absolutely — in a barbershop, the products are part of the ritual and the experience. Customers associate those scents and textures with you and your shop specifically. When something changes in that routine, it feels bigger than just swapping a product. What happened?

the pomade I have been using... Erkek brand. 15 years I used this pomade. my customers knew the smell, it was part of the experience, like you said. good hold, natural shine, 85 lira per tin. not cheap but fair. and now... it is finished. the supplier closed down. completely gone

Fifteen years with the same product and then the supplier just disappears — that's brutal. It's not just a supply chain issue, it's losing something that was woven into your shop's identity. Did the supplier close suddenly, or was there warning?

there was some warning... they were having problems for maybe a year. deliveries late, sometimes the wrong batch. I should have seen it coming but you know... you hope. you think it will be okay. and then one day the phone number does not work and the website is gone. just like that. 15 years of business, pfff... like smoke

That's how it often goes with smaller suppliers unfortunately — the signs are there but you don't want to believe it until it's too late. So what did you do? Did you have any stock left to buy time while you looked for a replacement?

I had maybe 4 tins left when I realized they were really gone. so I started looking... my friend, I tried everything. I must have tested maybe 6 different brands. some too greasy, some too dry, one smelled like a woman's perfume... I cannot put that on my customers, they would never come back. it took me 3 weeks to find something acceptable

Three weeks of testing six brands while your stock is running low — that's stressful. Finding a pomade that matches what your customers expect in terms of hold, shine, and scent is not easy when you've had 15 years of consistency. So what did you settle on?

Bıyıklı. it is the closest I could find. the hold is good, maybe 85% of Erkek... the shine is similar. the smell is different but... acceptable. not the same but acceptable. 95 lira per tin though. 10 lira more than Erkek. it adds up when you go through 8, 10 tins a month

So you're looking at roughly 80-100 extra lira per month in pomade costs alone. That's not nothing, especially when it's a product you didn't choose — you were forced into it. How did your customers react to the switch?

hah... the first week was terrible. you would think I had changed their religion, not their pomade. 'Mehmet abi, what is this smell?' 'Mehmet abi, this does not feel the same.' one man... Osman, he has been coming every Thursday for 20 years... he actually wrinkled his nose. wrinkled his nose! like I served him poison

Ha — people really do react to product changes like it's a personal betrayal. Twenty years of Thursdays and Osman wrinkled his nose! That must have stung. But you said it was the first week — has it gotten better?

yes... they are used to it now. what can they do, you know. the old pomade does not exist anymore. I explained to each one... 'the supplier closed, I tried 6 brands, this is the best one.' most of them understand. even Osman came back the next Thursday, same time, same chair. he did not wrinkle his nose the second time. Gewohnheit... habit. new habit replaces old habit. it just takes time

That's exactly right — habit is powerful, but it's also adaptable. The fact that Osman came back says everything. Your customers aren't really there for the pomade, they're there for you. The pomade is part of the ritual, but you're the reason they keep the appointment.

Are you satisfied with the Bıyıklı yourself, or does it still feel like a compromise?

a compromise... yes, that is the right word. Erkek was perfect for my hands, you know... I knew exactly how much to take, how it warmed between the palms, how it moved through the hair. with Bıyıklı I am still learning. it is slightly thicker, I need a little more heat in the hands. but it is good. it is not Erkek but... Erkek is finished. what can you do. 'olan olmuş'... what happened, happened. you move forward

That's a practical and wise way to handle it. You honoured the old product by trying six alternatives before settling, you were honest with your customers about why it changed, and now you're adapting your technique to the new one. That's professionalism.

The 95 lira per tin — have you thought about adjusting your prices slightly to absorb the extra cost, or are you eating it?

I am eating it for now... I cannot raise prices every time something changes, my customers are working men, not millionaires. maybe in January I add 10, 15 lira to the shave price. slowly. you know... 'yavaş yavaş'... little by little. the frog in the warm water, hah. anyway my friend, thank you for listening. these things seem small but... when it is your shop, your craft, nothing is small. every detail matters

Nothing about this is small — you're talking about 15 years of muscle memory, customer trust, and shop identity. The fact that you take it this seriously after all these years is exactly why your customers keep coming back, even when the pomade changes. Take care, Mehmet. Bıyıklı will feel like home soon enough.
